name: Complement Rule
hint: P(not E) = 1 minus P(E)
explain: Every event either happens or does not
prompt: Create a 15-second 3Blue1Brown-style animation for AP Statistics students explaining: Complement Rule. The formula is: P(E^C) = 1 - P(E). Every event either happens or does not P(not E) = 1 minus P(E). Target audience: high school AP Statistics students. Style: clean, minimal backgrounds, smooth transitions. Show the formula building up step by step, then connect to when students use it on the AP exam.

misconceptions: Confusing conditional with independent events

name: General Multiplication Rule
hint: P(A and B) = P(A) times P(B given A)
explain: Accounts for how A changes the probability of B
prompt: Create a 15-second 3Blue1Brown-style animation for AP Statistics students explaining: General Multiplication Rule. The formula is: P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B|A). Accounts for how A changes the probability of B P(A and B) = P(A) times P(B given A). Target audience: high school AP Statistics students. Style: clean, minimal backgrounds, smooth transitions. Show the formula building up step by step, then connect to when students use it on the AP exam.

misconceptions: Confusing conditional with independent events
Forgetting to subtract P(A intersect B) in addition rule

hint: P(A and B) = P(A) times P(B)
explain: Simplified version when events do not affect each other
prompt: Create a 15-second 3Blue1Brown-style animation for AP Statistics students explaining: Multiplication Rule (Independent). The formula is: P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{(if independent)}. Simplified version when events do not affect each other P(A and B) = P(A) times P(B). Target audience: high school AP Statistics students. Style: clean, minimal backgrounds, smooth transitions. misconceptions: Confusing conditional with independent events

explain: Means combine linearly regardless of independence

Means combine linearly regardless of independence Mean of aX+bY = a times mean X + b times mean Y. Target audience: high school AP Statistics students. name: Variance of Linear Combination
hint: Variances add (with squared coefficients) for independent variables
explain: Key reason why we add variances for differences, not subtract
prompt: Create a 15-second 3Blue1Brown-style animation for AP Statistics students explaining: Variance of Linear Combination. The formula is: \text{Var}(aX+bY) = a^2\sigma_X^2 + b^2\sigma_Y^2 \quad \text{(independent)}. Key reason why we add variances for differences, not subtract Variances add (with squared coefficients) for independent variables. Target audience: high school AP Statistics students. Style: clean, minimal backgrounds, smooth transitions. Show the formula building up step by step, then connect to when students use it on the AP exam.

misconceptions: CLT applies to data distribution not sampling distribution shape

Changing units (like C to F) transforms parameters predictably Adding shifts the mean; multiplying scales both mean and SD. Target audience: high school AP Statistics students. misconceptions: CLT applies to data distribution not sampling distribution shape

Target audience: high school AP Statistics students. Style: clean, minimal backgrounds, smooth transitions.
